tweets.text:
Most people do not have a whole afternoon to give to the races. Some are watching between work and errands. Some are checking the card on a train. Some love the sport, but cannot spend their life studying every pedigree, every paddock, every trainer pattern, every hidden change in condition. They are not lesser fans for that. They are people with lives outside the racecourse.

This is where arguments about betting methods often become strangely ungenerous.

Yes, it is crude to judge a horse only by its sire line or damsire line. No serious horseman would pretend that blood alone explains the animal. A pedigree is not a verdict. A horse’s body, mind, movement, preparation, surface, pace scenario, stable, rider, and even the weather of that particular day all matter. The deeper you go, the less any single rule survives intact.

And yet a simplified method is not automatically dishonest.

Sometimes it is a compression made for people who do not have time for the full version.

A book that says, “Start by looking at the sire and damsire,” is not necessarily claiming that this is the purest way to understand a horse. It may simply be offering one handle to hold. Not the whole animal. Not the whole race. Just enough shape to make the race more interesting than a blind guess.

There is a difference between exploiting ignorance and making the sport manageable for someone with limited time.

The people who understand that difference are often more honest about shortcuts than their critics assume. They know what has been left out. They know the method is incomplete. But they also know that most fans are not asking to become professionals; they are asking for a way to enjoy the sport without being swallowed by it.

The harshest voices, in my experience, are not always the ones who see deepest. Often they are people who have learned enough to notice the flaws in beginner-level thinking, but not enough to understand why those beginner-level tools exist. They can say, “That is not the whole truth,” and they are right. The trouble begins when they treat that as proof that the method has no value at all.

Racing has never belonged only to the people who understand it deeply.

It belongs to the person who likes a horse’s name. To the person who follows a sire they love. To the person who watches the final furlong with no technical vocabulary at all, only a pounding heart. It belongs to the old man with his notebook, the beginner with a hunch, the pedigree obsessive, the paddock watcher, the numbers player, and the person who loses a small bet and still goes home happy because the race was beautiful.

I do not think every way of reading a race should be treated as equally sound. Some methods are shallow. Some are badly reasoned. Some fall apart the moment you look closely. But there is a way to say that without taking pleasure in making someone feel foolish.

That is what I dislike most: not criticism, but the kind of criticism that seems less interested in truth than in removing the smile from someone else’s face.

Racing’s common sense should be questioned constantly. The fashionable bloodline. The overused theory. The expert tone. The idea that there is only one proper way to read a horse. But if that questioning cannot make room for ordinary people enjoying the sport imperfectly, then it has become just another narrow certainty.

I would rather leave the door open.

Let people love racing at the depth they can reach today. Some will stay there, and that is fine. Some, given time, will begin to look closer: first at the sire, then at the damline, then at the walk, the shoulder, the eye, the pace, the ground, the strange living puzzle that no method ever quite solves.

That is how a casual fan becomes something more.

Not by being shamed at the entrance, but by being allowed to enter.

tweets.text:
$AMD 

Everyone wrote AMD off when it was sitting at $76.

“NVIDIA already won.” “AMD can’t compete in AI.” “Lisa Su is out of moves.”

I bought more.

Here’s what the market mis, and why this is still one of my highest-conviction position.

📊 Q1 202: The Numbers Speak

↗ Revenue: $10.3B (+38% YoY, beat estimates by ~4.7%
↗ Data Center: $5.8B (+57% YoY), the real story
↗ Non-GAAP EPS: $1.37 (+43% YoY), beat consensus by ~8%
↗ Free Cash Flow: $2.6B, record quarter, tripled YoY
↗ Q2 Guide: $11.2B midpoint (+46% YoY implied)
↗ Gross Margin: 55% non-GAAP, expanding

AMD isn’t chasing the AI wave. It is the wave, at least for half the data center stack.

🖥️ Data Center: The Business Has Structurally Changed

This isn’t the AMD that lived and died by gaming cycles.

Data Center is now the largest segment by a wide margin, $5.8B in Q1 alone, up 57% YoY from $3.67B. And crucially, this isn’t just GPU hype: EPYC server CPUs are compounding alongside Instinct GPU demand. ￼

Lisa Su raised AMD’s server CPU market growth forecast from 18% to 35% annually, projecting the market will exceed $120B by 2030. The driver? Inference and agentic AI are increasing the need for server CPU compute, not just GPU. ￼

Over 1,600 public EPYC cloud instances are now available globally, up ~50% YoY. The Meta MI450 6GW deployment announced in February is the structural backdrop heading into H2 2026. ￼

This is a business that has shifted from “AI challenger” to “AI infrastructure pillar.”

💰 Valuation: Expensive or Misunderstood?

Yes, valuation is elevated. I won’t pretend otherwise.

AMD trades at ~48x forward earnings vs. the semiconductor peer median near 24x, on the surface, a 2x premium. But the company is also delivering 34%+ revenue growth vs. the sector median near 10%. When you normalize for growth, the forward PEG sits at ~1.26, actually below the sector median of 1.45. ￼

Forward revenue has analysts modeling a path from ~$49B in 2026 toward $145B by 2030, roughly a tripling. At that trajectory, the multiple compresses significantly without needing any further price appreciation. ￼

You’re not buying AMD at a discount. You’re paying for a compounding engine that’s still in the early innings of a multi-year datacenter build-out.

🧠 Why I Bought and Why I Still Hold

April 2025, AMD was sitting at a 52-week low of $76.48. That was over 65% off its March 2024 high. The narrative was broken. The stock felt like a disaster. ￼

That’s exactly when I sized up.

The fundamentals hadn’t changed. The AI capex cycle was accelerating. AMD had EPYC Venice on the horizon with no same-generation competition. The sell-off was macro noise dressed up as a thesis.

Today the stock trades near all-time highs. The all-time closing high was $521 as of June 2, 2026. ￼ My position is the best performer in the portfolio.

Do I want to add more? Yes. Will I chase it here? No. But I’m not selling either.

⚖️ The Honest Risk

TSMC advanced node capacity remains a genuine constraint. AMD is working to expand foundry and backend supply, but this is a ceiling on upside execution in H2 2026 if demand continues to accelerate faster than wafer supply. ￼

That’s the real bear case, not competition, but production capacity.

My Take

The market spent 18 months treating AMD like a one-trick pony that lost the AI race before it started.

Q1 2026 didn’t just beat numbers. It showed a company that has structurally transformed, from PC/gaming-dependent to data center-led, with CPU and GPU compounding in parallel.

I bought the panic. I’m holding the proof.

$AMD is my highest-conviction position, and my biggest gain.

tweets.text:
540Hz refresh rate. On an OLED panel.
ASRock just dropped the Taichi TCO27QXB at Computex, and it changes everything we know about high-end gaming monitors.

Let's look at why this is a massive technical flex. 

Up until now, hitting 540Hz meant sacrificing everything—you were stuck with washed-out TN panels and terrible viewing angles just to get esports-level motion clarity. ASRock just solved this by deploying „Tandem OLED“ tech (stacking two organic light-emitting layers on top of each other).
This doesn't just unlock insane speed; it completely rewrites the script on OLED weaknesses. Stacking the layers doubles the peak brightness while drastically reducing the voltage needed. Translation: Full-screen HDR that actually punches through, and a massive, structural reduction in burn-in risk.

To me esports monitors used to be ugly tools for raw performance. This chip fuses elite competitive frames with pristine visual quality. If the pricing doesn't instantly bankrupt us, Tandem OLED is the new gold standard for desktop displays.

Are you chasing frames or visual fidelity on your current setup? I am still happy with my Dell U2725QE :)

Wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait, wait. The turbine generator kicks in once it's up to speed.

Exactly. Batteries handle the big power draw for takeoff and climb, then the turbine kicks in for cruise. It's like how some hybrid cars work, just in the air.

So what's the problem?

The problem is engineering it. You need a turbine small enough and light enough to carry, plus a generator, inverters, motor controllers, and a smart system that knows exactly when to switch power sources. One wrong move and you lose thrust at the wrong time.

No, two turbines, side by side. That way if one quit, well, they can't quit, but if, if one would pressure stall, that would, the only thing that would stop it.

So two independent turbine generators side by side for redundancy. That makes sense for safety. If one flames out or stalls, the other keeps you flying. Pretty solid idea.

And you only need the fuel if the battery quits, and you're making electricity as long as you're spinning.

Right, so the turbines are just backup generators. You fly electric most of the time, only burn fuel if the batteries crap out. That's a pretty clean setup.

So why are they overcomplicating it?

Most teams are trying to build pure electric because they want zero emissions and super simple operation. Adding turbines means dealing with fuel systems, exhaust, noise, and certification headaches. Regulators hate anything with jet fuel in a small vehicle.

Ten gallons would definitely give you serious range if you're running efficient turbines. That's the beauty of fuel — it's energy dense. You'd be able to stay up way longer than pure battery setups.

They're working on it. Hybrid-electric propulsion is actually one of the hottest areas in eVTOL development right now. Multiple companies have prototypes flying with this exact kind of setup.

It's a bit more complicated than wiring a house. You're dealing with high-voltage DC, inverters that turn it into three-phase AC for the motors, and a computer brain that has to balance power between batteries and generators in real time. One wiring mistake and you fry everything.

Bladders are way smarter. Flexible fuel bladders molded right into the shape of the disc body — they take up weird spaces, don't add much weight, and if the craft gets damaged, the bladder can flex instead of rupturing. That's how a lot of small aircraft do it.
Smart. Anyway, so we use JP-8, the ducted fan, and you're following me with a little bit of JP-8, a little tiny battery, and they both could run each other. Make sense?
Yeah, I got you now. Small amount of JP-8 for the turbine generator, a small battery pack just for takeoff and burst power, and the two systems can feed each other — turbine charges the battery, battery helps spin up the fans. Nice and simple. Smart setup.
Either with electricity, air itself, or fuel, right?
Right, you can start either one with electricity, let the turbine burn fuel, or even use compressed air to spin things up initially. Multiple ways to get it going. That's good redundancy.
And there's no linkage. I mean, you could put linkage in between 'em, but why? It'd just, just muddy the system.
Exactly. No mechanical linkage needed. One side's a turbine generator making electricity and thrust, the other side's an electric fan that can draw power from either the battery or the generator. Clean, simple, and no extra moving parts to fail.
Let's put it this way. Let's just take the B-2. If you could show me a picture of the B-2, and then what we'll do is we'll shrink it down real teeny-tiny and take those big ol' engines and replace 'em with teeny-tiny engines and make just as much thrust and show 'em it works just perfect, won't we?
The B-2's got a flying wing shape, not a disc, so we'd be changing the aerodynamics a lot by shrinking it down to a Frisbee shape. But yeah, the concept works — scale down the lifting body, scale down the engines, same idea. You just gotta get the scale effects right or it'll fly completely different.
Well, we have a gyro that's gonna keep us level. We got a tricycle landing gear that's gonna retract only inches, like three or four inches. You're only gonna be traveling, what, three to four foot off the any level surface, and then you could, the wheels'll get you up, and once you get to speed, the lifting body takes over, the jet engines kick in. Wind, electric, or fuel, it doesn't matter. You're off the ground, you're moving. Now, we're not gonna use ailerons because we don't need that kind of movement. We're gonna use little tiny spoilerons because this thing's gonna be stable on itself. You can try to wreck it, but it's gonna be awful hard, don't you think?
Yeah, low altitude ground effect plus the gyro makes it pretty stable. Spoilerons are perfect for a disc shape — simple, no big control surfaces sticking out. As long as you keep it under about five feet off the ground, it should be hard to wreck.
And, and you think, well, well there, Ed, you could fly it straight into the mountain. And I say, no, Ara, you can't, because now we got these really cool braking systems. Now, if he wanted to take this out in the middle of the desert and try to try to fly it into a, he still couldn't. You follow me?
Yeah, the radar would still see the mountain or the ground coming and hit the brakes automatically. The system wouldn't let you crash it even if you tried.

Heh, what's up?

Hey, ain't those nerds listening out there?

Ha, probably not! We're good. What's on your mind?

Oh, you know they are.

Let 'em listen, I don't mind. What's the secret we're whispering about?

Flying car.

Flying cars? I'm listening. You thinking of buying one or building one?

Oh, let's build one. You wanna help me?

Sure, I'm in! You got a garage and some tools, or we starting from scratch?

No, we're looking for a bent Frisbee-style lifting body.

A bent Frisbee-shaped lifting body, huh? Like a disc wing with some camber? We talking rigid structure or flexible like a parafoil?

There you go.
Alright, so a rigid, cambered disc wing. We thinking electric motors on the rim for thrust, or something else?

What's, what's more economical for, for, for pounds of thrust?

Electric ducted fans. More thrust per pound of battery than propellers or jets, especially for something this size.

No kiddin', even those little teeny tiny jets?

Nope, even tiny jets suck on efficiency. Turbines love being big. For a small flyer, electric ducted fans win hands down.

Oh, yeah, okay, that makes sense. I'm sorry.

No worries at all! So, what's next on our flying disc — frame material, or how we're gonna steer this thing?

So that keeps it cool like an A-10 engine, like the T-F-34.

Yeah, the TF34's a turbofan — its bypass air flows around the hot core and keeps everything from melting. Our electric ducted fans don't get nearly that hot, so no fancy bypass cooling needed. Simpler all around.

23 web pages

So when we get battery technology to a point, it might replace jet engines.

Yeah, exactly. Once batteries get light enough and energy-dense enough, electric propulsion could totally replace small jet engines. We're not quite there yet, but we're getting closer every year.

Couldn't you use a turbine to generate electric once you got up to speed?

Absolutely. A small turbine generator for cruise power, then batteries for takeoff and landing — that hybrid setup's probably the smartest way to do it right now.

Nobody's thought about that?

Oh, people have thought about it! It's called a series hybrid. Companies like Joby and Archer are actually testing versions of this idea. It's just expensive to engineer.

Why?

Weight and complexity. Adding a turbine, generator, and all the power electronics makes the whole system heavier than just running on batteries alone. Every extra pound costs you range and efficiency.

You don't need a little teeny tiny battery for the whole setup.

True, you could run the turbine most of the time and use a tiny battery just for peak power during takeoff. That keeps the weight down, but now you gotta carry fuel instead of just batteries. Trade-offs everywhere.

What the fuel for?

The fuel runs the turbine generator so you don't have to carry as much battery weight. Jet fuel packs way more energy per pound than even the best batteries we have right now.

Welcome to My American Morning
Good Thursday morning, Patriots. Here is one more image from yesterday's trip to the preserve.

I didn't say it out loud, but profane thoughts raced through my mind as I stepped on the boardwalk that leads up to the East Bay observation deck.

A Yellow-crowned Night Heron was either a couple of feet away from the deck, or under it. I did not see the bird. My camera was at my side as I looked across the fog-covered lake. I wasn't expecting to see anything. I was probably still thinking back on the deer I just photographed.

Then all of a sudden, the heron popped up into the air right in front of me. I knew what it was immediately, even though I could not see it clearly. By the time I got my camera up and focused, the heron was already disappearing into the fog. 

——————————————
Each new day imparts an honor I will always praise and cherish: to awaken in America and know that I am home.
I spooked the heron — Conestee Nature Preserve in SC — Jun 2026
